claims.statement: A single s5cmd ls issues one serial ListObjectsV2 continuation chain per source URL and consumes it with a plain serial loop, with no worker pool or fan-out over the listing.
claims.qualification: Source establishes the serial chain; a captured --log trace of one prefix corroborates it at runtime as one continuation-token chain, but that probe is not a smoke-run receipt and binds only that one ls.

claims.statement: s5cmd performs no keyspace bisection, cut-point, or prefix-sharding to parallelize a listing; a wildcard becomes one recursive List plus a client-side regex filter.
claims.qualification: The prefix sent to S3 is only the literal portion before the first glob character; the wildcard tail is filtered client-side per key.

claims.statement: Whether the serial single-stream listing model dominates wall-clock at 10^6 to 10^8 keys, and how it compares against parallel-capable tools, is not settled.
claims.qualification: Serial-versus-parallel behavior at scale is a timing comparison outside smoke scope; source reading is not a receipt and no benchmark has run.

evidence.reason: No comparative or at-scale benchmark of the listing chain has been run.

claims.statement: The LIST request and its pagination are issued in storage/s3.go, while command/ls.go only consumes the resulting object channel.
claims.qualification: The inherited page named command/ls.go as where the serial LIST lived; groundwork located the request and pagination in storage/s3.go instead.

claims.statement: For a single ls the worker pool has no effect on the LIST chain; the pool parallelizes only the downstream cp/rm/mv operations.
claims.qualification: The blanket claim that all parallelism is transfer-side holds for a lone ls but not for s5cmd run, which dispatches its ls lines through the same pool; see claim numworkers-sizes-run-fanout-concurrency.

claims.statement: s5cmd's published speed reputation and its own benchmarks describe transfer throughput driven by a worker pool, not listing.
claims.qualification: This is a documentation fact about upstream's own material; the transfer speed itself is out of listing scope and was not measured here.

claims.statement: The magnitude of s5cmd ls speed relative to aws s3api is not settled.
claims.qualification: This is a timing comparison outside smoke scope; the inherited 1.5x figure was itself a self-reported comparison in the seed notes and was never run here.

evidence.reason: No comparative timing run against aws s3api has been performed.

claims.statement: A native recursive s5cmd ls of the full smoke bucket runs and completes.
claims.qualification: The receipted full-bucket recursive run listed 148,917 keys and passed the verifier.

claims.statement: The output flags -e (etag), -H (humanize), and --json change only the printed format and do not change the ListObjectsV2 request or listing mechanism.
claims.qualification: The JSON mode was smoked and produces the same key set as recursive; the text flags are source-established as formatting-only.

claims.statement: A hand-built per-prefix fan-out of s5cmd ls invocations, unioned over four prefix shards plus the unprefixed remainder, covers the smoke bucket completely with no duplicates.
claims.qualification: The --scope union verification passed at 148,917 keys with 0 cross-shard duplicates, 0 missing, and 0 extra; this settles completeness only, not speed.

claims.statement: s5cmd v2.3.0 run takes its commands file positionally or on stdin and has no -f flag.
claims.qualification: The inherited page called for run -f <file>; run -f fails with an unknown-flag error and exit 1, observed in the run-fanout probe.

claims.statement: --numworkers sizes how many command lines s5cmd run executes concurrently, so it governs the fan-out's prefix-listing concurrency even though it does not affect a single ls.
claims.qualification: The default is 256; it is the fan-out's concurrency knob, not a listing knob for a lone ls.

claims.statement: The effect of sweeping --numworkers on fan-out listing throughput is not settled.
claims.qualification: The sweep is a benchmark-phase question; smoke produces no comparative numbers.

claims.statement: Whether s5cmd cp/sync transfer speed separates fast transfers from fast listing is not settled here.
claims.qualification: cp/sync are out of listing scope and mutating; the study guardrail forbids running them, so they were not smoked.

evidence.reason: cp/sync were not run; they are out of listing scope and mutating.

claims.statement: The inherited survey figure of 15M objects in 733 s at about 20.5K objects per second is not settled.
claims.qualification: This is a scale and timing claim; the smoke bucket is 148,917 keys and produces no comparative numbers.

evidence.reason: No at-scale timing run has been performed.

claims.statement: The inherited report of about 11 GB RAM syncing 400k files, and the broader low-resource-use characterization, is not settled.
claims.qualification: The report concerns sync at scale, not listing, and was not reproduced; the listing-path smoke memory is recorded separately as ls-streaming-memory-at-smoke.

evidence.reason: The sync memory report was not reproduced; sync is out of listing scope.

claims.statement: Streaming ls held a low peak RSS at smoke, about 40 MB for the full recursive listing and about 53 MB for the all-versions listing on the 148,917-key bucket.
claims.qualification: These are peak-RSS facts from single smoke runs, consistent with the source-established streaming model, not a scaling claim above 148,917 keys.

claims.statement: The inherited report of s5cmd being killed at 15M objects is not settled.
claims.qualification: This is a scale claim; it was not attempted against the 148,917-key smoke bucket.

evidence.reason: The 15M-object scale scenario was not attempted.

claims.statement: The 2025 re-testing pointer for s5cmd's published speed results (a Hacker News thread versus a BigGo aggregator) is not settled.
claims.qualification: Neither source has been read, and both concern transfer speed outside listing scope; it remains a pointer, not a result.

evidence.reason: Neither the Hacker News thread nor the BigGo aggregator has been read; both concern transfer speed.

claims.statement: s5cmd supports glob and wildcard listing, applied as a client-side filter over a recursive List.
claims.qualification: Scoped globs were smoked and passed; the wildcard is compiled to a regex applied per key as pages arrive.

claims.statement: The speed of the hand-built fan-out relative to a native parallel lister is not settled.
claims.qualification: Fan-out completeness is confirmed separately; its comparative speed is a benchmark-phase question.

evidence.reason: No comparative speed run of the fan-out against a native parallel lister has been performed.

claims.statement: s5cmd never sets MaxKeys, so S3 returns its own 1,000-key page maximum, which is S3's own ceiling rather than an s5cmd deficit.
claims.qualification: The inherited page framed 1,000 as an s5cmd disadvantage; because 1,000 is S3's hard ceiling, no real-S3 client can exceed it, and it only matters versus tools that parallelize pages across sharded prefixes.

claims.statement: s5cmd publishes prebuilt binaries per release across the supported platforms.
claims.qualification: The release pipeline targets amd64 and arm64 for Linux, macOS, and Windows; groundwork itself ran the upstream published container image rather than a bare binary.

claims.statement: --log trace writes the full AWS SDK request and response records to stdout, so the per-request page count is obtainable even though s5cmd exposes no built-in numeric counter.
claims.qualification: An earlier probe wrongly concluded there was no per-request logging by checking stderr; --log trace writes to stdout, where a 3-page trace shows one HeadBucket probe and sequential ListObjectsV2 pages. --stat only tallies operations and --log debug emits nothing per-request. Correction history is in research/codex-review.md.

claims.statement: Every smoked listing mode ran unsigned against the public bucket and passed completeness and correctness.
claims.qualification: All modes ran --no-sign-request against noaa-normals-pds at its 2026-07-17 snapshot; these are single-run groundwork facts, not benchmark results.

claims.statement: The study's normalize.sh text branches split on whitespace and rejoin fields with a single space, so a key containing runs of spaces, tabs, or an embedded newline is not reproduced byte-for-byte.
claims.qualification: This is a limitation of the study adapter, exact for the whitespace-free NOAA smoke keyspace; general weird-key fidelity is deferred with the edge-case fixture (EDGE_BUCKET=none).

claims.statement: The all-versions smoke PASS validates that s5cmd issues the ListObjectVersions request and produces the expected output shape on a non-versioned bucket.
claims.qualification: The adapter discards the trailing versionID token, which is sound only because the smoke bucket is non-versioned.

claims.statement: Multi-version and delete-marker fidelity of the all-versions mode is not settled.
claims.qualification: On a versioned bucket multiple versions of one key would collapse into duplicate records; this needs a versioned fixture and is deferred with the edge cases (EDGE_BUCKET=none).

evidence.reason: No versioned-bucket fixture was configured or run.

claims.statement: Every prefix-scoped receipt.md renders its Prefix scope cell twice, a cosmetic wrapper-template defect.
claims.qualification: The wrapper bug was fixed harness-side in commit 014f74a; run.meta, which the verifier reads, was always correct, so no verdict was affected. The already-committed receipts keep the malformed cell as an accurate record of what the wrapper rendered.
